summary: DHH challenges the logical fallacy of dismissing new approaches because similar past attempts failed. He uses HEY's #NoBuild JavaScript approach as a concrete example — running without bundling or compiling JavaScript for three years with tens of thousands of paying customers and a perfect Lighthouse performance score. He argues that progress happens when people do things differently, and that import maps (available since 2020) changed what's feasible. Rather than evangelizing, he shares HEY's approach as proof that simplicity works, especially for small teams that benefit from conceptual compression.
key_points: The 'we tried that, didn't work' fallacy wrongly limits collective ambition based on others' past failures
HEY has run without JavaScript bundling or compiling for three years, achieving a perfect 100/100 Lighthouse performance score
Import maps (post-2020) changed what's technically feasible, invalidating mental models formed in the 2010-2020 era
Progress requires doing something different from predecessors, not dismissing new approaches based on outdated assumptions
Conceptual compression — removing entire steps from the development process — is especially valuable for small teams
money_quotes: In our quest for making programming simpler, faster, and prettier, no logical fallacy provides as much of an obstacle as "we tried that, didn't work".
There would be no human progress if we all quit trying after any unsuccessful attempt.
It's like insisting your map is correct even after it failed to record the massive mountain you're literally staring at.
Refusing to believe reality because it doesn't comport to your mental model of the world is an intellectual failure state.
That's how progress usually happens! By someone doing something different than whoever went before them in pursuit of the same goal.
Making programming better requires a willingness to test your priors. To question your assumptions. To recognize the half-life of facts.
Nothing compresses what you need to know like removing an entire step from the equation.
Don't get stuck in the mud of complexity.
key_insight: Past failures don't define the boundaries of what's possible — technologies evolve, and dismissing proven results because they contradict your outdated mental model is an intellectual failure state.
